revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:館林の牛頭天王信仰]]
== Gozu Tennō Faith in Tatebayashi ==
'''Gozu Tennō Faith in Tatebayashi''' (館林の牛頭天王信仰) refers to the {{ill|Gozu Tennō|en|Gozu Tennō|qid=Q11570247|lt=Gozu Tennō信仰|1=Gozu Tennō}}, a branch of {{ill|Gion cult|en|Gion cult|qid=Q11588425|lt=Gion Faith|1=Gion Faith}}, that was widely practiced in the {{ill|Castle Town of Tatebayashi|ja|館林城下町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Castle Town of Tatebayashi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, located in {{ill|Tatebayashi, Gunma|en|Tatebayashi, Gunma|qid=Q858016|lt=Tatebayashi, Gunma|1=Tatebayashi, Gunma}}, {{ill|Gunma Prefecture|en|Gunma Prefecture|qid=Q129499|lt=Gunma Prefecture|1=Gunma Prefecture}}{{sfn|館林市誌 歴史編|pp=847-849,851-854,869}}{{sfn|館林市史 普及版|pp=114-115,126-127}}{{sfn|館林懐古|pp=116-117}}{{sfn|尾曳之跡|pp=35-36,171-174}}.
It originated during the {{ill|Tenshō (Momoyama period)|en|Tenshō (Momoyama period)|qid=Q1145058|lt=Tenshō Era|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}}, when {{ill|Gozu Tennō|en|Gozu Tennō|qid=Q11570247|lt=Gozu Tennō|1=Gozu Tennō}} was enshrined as the {{ill|Market God|ja|市神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Market God|qq=}} for the {{ill|Rokusai Markets|ja|六斎市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokusai Markets|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 六斎日|1=Rokusai Markets|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q24897642}} that began in {{ill|Renjaku-chō (Tatebayashi)|ja|連雀町 (館林市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Renjaku-chō (Tatebayashi)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}{{sfn|館林市誌 歴史編|pp=847-849}}. Later, the faith was spread across three different locations: {{ill|Ashikaga-chō (Tatebayashi)|ja|足利町 (館林市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashikaga-chō (Tatebayashi)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Yagoe-chō|ja|谷越町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yagoe-chō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Tate-machi (Tatebayashi)|ja|竪町 (館林市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tate-machi (Tatebayashi)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}{{sfn|館林市誌 歴史編|pp=851-854}}. The summer festivals of these locations evolved into what is now known as the {{ill|Tatebayashi Festival|ja|館林まつり|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tatebayashi Festival|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}{{sfn|館林市史 普及版|p=127}}, forming the mainstream lineage of town folk devotion.
Additionally, in the late {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|qid=Q184963|lt=Edo Period|1=Edo period}}, when the {{ill|Akimoto Clan|ja|秋元氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akimoto Clan|1=Akimoto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11594852}} was transferred to Tatebayashi, two shrines of {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|ja|八坂神社 (曖昧さ回避)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yasaka Shrine|qq=}} from the castle town of {{ill|Yamagata Castle|en|Yamagata Castle|qid=Q15604669|lt=Yamagata Castle|1=Yamagata Castle}} were relocated to {{ill|Soto-Kahōshi|ja|外加法師|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Soto-Kahōshi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Soto-Bangi|ja|外伴木|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Soto-Bangi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, where they served as guardian shrines for certain retainers of the Akimoto family{{sfn|尾曳之跡|pp=35-36}}.
== History ==
=== Invitation of the Market Deity to Renjaku Town === The {{ill|market|en|market|qid=Q1277907|lt=market|1=Market}} (市) in [[:en:Tatebayashi|Tatebayashi]] had been interrupted during the {{ill|Sengoku period|en|Sengoku period|qid=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period|1=Sengoku period}} due to war, but it was reopened under the rule of the {{ill|Later Hōjō clan|en|Later Hōjō clan|qid=Q431928|lt=Later Hōjō clan|1=Later Hōjō clan}}{{sfn|Tatebayashi City History, Popular Edition|p=114}}. According to the ''[[Tatebayashi Chronicle]]'' (館林記), in December of [[:en:Tenshō|Tenshō]] 12 (1584), during the time when {{ill|Hōjō Ujinori|en|Hōjō Ujinori|qid=Q1968898|lt=Hōjō Ujinori|1=Hōjō Ujinori}} was the lord of {{ill|Tatebayashi Castle|en|Tatebayashi Castle|qid=Q11666959|lt=Tatebayashi Castle|1=Tatebayashi Castle}}, a {{ill|Uirō|en|Uirō|qid=Q2984414|lt=Uirō|1=Uirō}} medicine seller from {{ill|Odawara Castle|en|Odawara Castle|qid=Q1013437|lt=Odawara|1=Odawara Castle}} took lodging at the home of Kodera Tango, who served as a [[kendan]] (market official){{sfn|Tatebayashi City Historical Records|p=847}}{{sfn|Tatebayashi City History, Popular Edition|p=114}}.
Tango consulted the merchant about the former ''Rokusaiichi'' (六斎市, markets held on six specific days of the month), which had ceased, and mentioned that nearby villages hoped to see its revival{{sfn|Tatebayashi City Historical Records|p=847}}{{sfn|Tatebayashi City History, Popular Edition|p=114}}. The merchant returned to Odawara and reported this to {{ill|Hōjō Ujinao|en|Hōjō Ujinao|qid=Q1156668|lt=Hōjō Ujinao|1=Hōjō Ujinao}}, who summoned Iijima Gen'emon and Suzuki Hanbei from Tatebayashi to confirm the origins of the market and the market deity. After their explanation, Ujinao granted permission for the establishment of the market and the enshrinement of a market deity{{sfn|Tatebayashi City History, Popular Edition|p=114}}{{sfn|Tatebayashi Stories|pp=31-32}}.
As a result, a '''"Three-Eight Rokusaiichi"'''<ref group="notes">Markets were held six times a month on the 3rd, 8th, 13th, 18th, 23rd, and 28th.</ref> was established in '''Renjaku Town''', and the '''Yasaka Shrine''' (八坂神社), dedicated to [[Gozu Tennō]], was enshrined there as the market deity{{sfn|Tatebayashi City Historical Records|pp=847-848}}. Among the two summoned men, Suzuki was assigned as a general wholesaler (問屋) for commercial goods, while Iijima was designated as the head of the '''Renjaku merchants''', responsible for all matters related to the market deity{{sfn|Tatebayashi City Historical Records|pp=847-848}}.
=== Relocation of the Market Deity to the Three Towns === As the '''Rokusaiichi''' expanded beyond Renjaku Town, the market gradually shifted locations, and by the '''Enpō era''', it had settled into a '''rotation system''' among '''Tate-machi''', '''Ashikaga-machi''', and '''Yagoshi-machi'''{{refnest|group=notes|According to the ''Tatebayashi Town Regulations Memorandum'' (館林町先規の次第覚書), the market was held in Tate-machi on the 3rd and 18th, Yagoshi-machi on the 8th and 23rd, and Ashikaga-machi on the 13th and 28th{{sfn|Fukuda Keisaku Historical Materials|p=80}}.}}{{sfn|Tatebayashi Nostalgia|p=60}}{{sfn|Historical Changes in Tatebayashi Castle Town and Regional Structure|p=31}}.
As the market locations changed, the '''Gozu Tennō deity''' was also '''gradually relocated''' to these three towns. The exact timing of this relocation is unclear. By '''Hōei 5 (1708)''', shrine festivals were already being conducted at Yasaka Shrines in the three towns{{sfn|Tatebayashi City Historical Records|p=851}}, but another record indicates that the Renjaku Town market deity was still in place as late as '''Hōreki 7 (1757)'''{{sfn|Tatebayashi City Historical Records|p=848}}. It is likely that the '''relocation process was gradual''', with new enshrinements occurring alongside market shifts beginning in the '''Keichō period'''{{refnest|group=notes|In Enpō-era maps, the Ashikaga-machi shrine is labeled as a small "Tennō," but by the Akimoto era, the "Tatebayashi Castle Map" depicts it as a large shrine within Mitsuzō-in Temple's grounds{{sfn|Historical Changes in Tatebayashi Castle Town and Regional Structure|p=31}}.}}{{sfn|Tatebayashi City Historical Records|p=851}}.
====== ==== Locations of Yasaka Shrines in the Three Towns: ======
'''1. Tate-machi Tennō Shrine''' * Located within the grounds of [[Hōjō-in]] (法性院), the '''Bettō-ji''' (managing temple) in [[Zaimoku-machi]]{{refnest|group=notes|Originally located in Tate-machi but later moved to Zaimoku-machi{{sfn|Tatebayashi City Historical Records|p=851}}.}}{{sfn|Tatebayashi City Historical Records|p=851}}. * '''Sub-shrine''': [[Kotohira shrines|Kotohira Shrine]] (琴平神社){{sfn|Ōra Tatebayashi Historical Records|p=163}}. * Present-day '''Tatebayashi City, Naka-machi 8''', near '''Tajingū Shrine'''.
'''2. Yagoshi-machi Tennō Shrine''' * Located within the grounds of [[Enzō-in]] (円蔵院){{refnest|group=notes|A [[Shingon Buddhism|Shingon]] temple affiliated with [[Henshō-ji]] (遍照寺) in Shinjuku-mura{{sfn|Tatebayashi Temples and Shrines|p=35}}.}}. * '''Sub-shrine''': [[Sarutahiko Shrine]]{{sfn|Ōra Tatebayashi Historical Records|p=162}}. * Present-day '''Tatebayashi City, Naka-machi 4''', adjacent to '''Aome Shrine'''.
'''3. Ashikaga-machi Tennō Shrine''' * Located within the grounds of '''Mitsuzō-in''' (密蔵院){{sfn|Tatebayashi City Historical Records|p=851}}. * '''Sub-shrines''': [[Kotohira shrines|Kotohira Shrine]], {{ill|Inari shrine|en|Inari shrine|qid=Q514480|lt=Inari shrine|1=Inari shrine}}, [[Mitsumine Shrine]], and '''Chōnyūshi Shrine'''{{refnest|group=notes|'''Chōnyūshi Shrine''' enshrines the deity '''Michinaga Chōnyūshi-no-Kami''', a rare deity associated with child-rearing in Kyushu, but uncommon in this region{{sfn|Tatebayashi Temples and Shrines|p=12}}.}}. * Present-day '''Tatebayashi City, Honmachi 1-8''', near '''Kotohira Shrine'''.
=== Relocation from Yamagata === The final '''daimyō family''' of the '''Tatebayashi Domain''', the '''Akimoto clan''', was transferred from the '''Yamagata Domain'''. Upon their '''relocation in Kōka 3 (1846)''', some of their retainers transferred two '''Yasaka Shrines''' from '''Yamagata Castle''' to '''Sotokahōshi''' and '''Sototomoki''' in Tatebayashi{{sfn|Obiki no Ato|p=35}}. Although these shrines were private and not officially recognized as '''domain shrines''', they held deep religious significance among the samurai families{{sfn|Obiki no Ato|p=36}}.
====== ====== Locations of the Transferred Yasaka Shrines: ======
'''1. Sotokahōshi Yasaka Shrine''' * Originally enshrined at the '''Karuguchi Gate''' of '''Yamagata Castle'''{{sfn|Obiki no Ato|p=35}}. * Present-day '''Tatebayashi City, Obiki-chō 14''', near '''Tatebayashi Municipal Third Elementary School'''.
'''2. Sototomoki Yasaka Shrine''' * Originally enshrined at '''Tōkamachi Gate''' of '''Yamagata Castle'''{{sfn|Obiki no Ato|p=35}}. * '''Jointly enshrined''' with '''Kenshō Inari'''{{sfn|Obiki no Ato|p=35}}. * Present-day '''Tatebayashi City, Obiki-chō 26'''.
== Tennō Festival ==
=== The Tennō Festival of the Three Towns === The Tennō Festival, a festival of the Tennō shrines in three towns, has been held annually on {{ill|June 7 (lunar calendar)|ja|6月7日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/June 7 (lunar calendar)|1=June 7 (Lunisolar)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q839725}}{{sfn|館林市誌 歴史編|p=851}}.
==== Tennō Festival During the Rule of Tsunayoshi ==== According to the "Tatebayashi Machisaki no Shidai Memorandum" from {{ill|Hōei 5 (1708)|ja|宝永5年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōei 5 (1708)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135504462|1=1729}}, the Tennō Festival during the time when {{ill|Tokugawa Tsunayoshi|en|Tokugawa Tsunayoshi|qid=Q313745|lt=Tokugawa Tsunayoshi|1=Tokugawa Tsunayoshi}} was the lord of {{ill|Tatebayashi Domain|en|Tatebayashi Domain|qid=Q3034548|lt=Tatebayashi Domain|1=Tatebayashi Domain}} was as follows{{sfn|館林市誌 歴史編|pp=851-852}}:
* On {{ill|June 1 (Lunisolar)|ja|6月1日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/June 1 (Lunisolar)|qq=}}, seven days before the festival, materials were provided by the domain{{refnest|group="Note"|The materials from the domain consisted of six loads of green reed per shrine, 50 bamboo poles, four poles of five-sun circumference, and two {{ill|Gohei|en|Gohei|qid=Q1011372|lt=gohei|1=Gohei}} paper booklets per shrine{{sfn|館林市誌 歴史編|p=852}}. Since there were three shrines, three times this amount was actually supplied by the domain{{sfn|館林市誌 歴史編|p=852}}. The bamboo and green reed were used for making the roofs of temporary structures, and the pole bamboo was used for making the {{ill|Shishinhoko|ja|四神鉾|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shishinhoko|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a type of decorated spear with banners depicting the {{ill|Four Symbols|en|Four Symbols|qid=Q1073260|lt=Four Symbols|1=Four Symbols}} of Chinese mythology{{sfn|館林市誌 歴史編|p=852}}.}} and three {{ill|Otabisho|en|Otabisho|qid=Q11489117|lt=Otabisho|1=Otabisho}} were constructed in the town{{sfn|館林市誌 歴史編|pp=851-852}}. * On the festival day, the three shrines' {{ill|Mikoshi|en|Mikoshi|qid=Q979940|lt=mikoshi|1=Mikoshi}} were carried in a procession through the town, accompanied by {{ill|Yamabushi|en|Yamabushi|qid=Q2309838|lt=yamabushi|1=Yamabushi}}, {{ill|Miko|en|Miko|qid=Q1047139|lt=miko|1=Miko}}, {{ill|sacred horses|ja|神馬|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/sacred horses|1=Shinme|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11591047}}, {{ill|Cleyera japonica|en|Cleyera japonica|qid=Q775470|lt=sakaki|1=Cleyera japonica}}, {{ill|Lion dance|en|Lion dance|qid=Q713866|lt=shishi-mai|1=Lion dance}}, {{ill|Drum|en|Drum|qid=Q11404|lt=taiko|1=Drum}}, and {{ill|Matsuri float|en|Matsuri float|qid=Q11470974|lt=hoko|1=Matsuri float}}. The three mikoshi met at {{ill|Katamachi (Tatebayashi)|ja|片町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katamachi (Tatebayashi)|1=Katamachi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11569929}} {{sfn|館林市誌 歴史編|p=852}}. * The procession entered the {{ill|Ōtemon|ja|大手門|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtemon|1=ōte-mon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q5371967}}, crossed the {{ill|Sengan Bridge|ja|千貫橋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengan Bridge|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and proceeded into the castle, where the lord and castle administrator paid their respects, and {{ill|Kagura|en|Kagura|qid=Q1348260|lt=kagura|1=Kagura}} was offered{{sfn|館林市誌 歴史編|p=852}}. Offerings were given by the castle administrator{{refnest|group="Note"|The offerings included an initial harvest fee of one {{ill|kanmon|ja|貫文|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kanmon|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 貫|1=Japanese kan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q116911247}} per shrine, a sacred horse fee equivalent to two {{ill|hiki|ja|疋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hiki|1=hiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11578871}} per shrine, totaling three times that amount in reality{{sfn|館林市誌 歴史編|p=852}}. Additionally, the domain granted six {{ill|tawara|ja|俵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tawara|1=tawara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11386402}} of rice for offerings, two per shrine{{sfn|館林市誌 歴史編|p=852}}. Of these, three contained 3 {{ill|to|ja|斗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/to|1=To (unit)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1078168}} 7 {{ill|Sheng (volume)|en|Sheng (volume)|qid=Q995076|lt=shō|1=Sheng (volume)}} of glutinous rice{{sfn|館林市誌 歴史編|p=852}}. The glutinous rice was for offerings, while the regular rice was for food or making {{ill|Amazake|en|Amazake|qid=Q455990|lt=amazake|1=Amazake}} for the festival{{sfn|館林市誌 歴史編|p=852}}. The amount of these grants was highest during Tsunayoshi’s period and was significantly reduced under the {{ill|Ochi-Matsudaira clan|ja|越智松平家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ochi-Matsudaira clan|1=Ochi-Matsudaira clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11636553}}, although the festival continued in form until the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|en|Meiji Restoration|qid=Q8707|lt=Meiji Restoration|1=Meiji Restoration}}{{sfn|館林市誌 歴史編|p=852}}.}}{{sfn|館林市誌 歴史編|p=852}}. * After the lord's worship, the mikoshi left the castle and processed through the town{{sfn|館林市誌 歴史編|p=852}}.
According to the "Tatebayashi Machisashidashino Ushi Gozu Tennō Ikkensho Kajo" from {{ill|Kyōhō 14 (1729)|ja|享保14年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyōhō 14 (1729)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135504464|1=1729}}, the following additional elements were present{{sfn|館林市誌 歴史編|p=852}}: * Performances of {{ill|jiodori|ja|地踊り|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/jiodori|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|tsukurimono|ja|作り物|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tsukurimono|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|nerimono|ja|練り物|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/nerimono|1=nerimono|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11608011}} were held. On the festival eve, June 6, performances were given before the two magistrates, and on the main festival day, they were presented inside the castle before returning to the town{{sfn|館林市誌 歴史編|p=852}}. * The castle was cleaned, and {{ill|yatai kyōgen|ja|屋台狂言|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/yatai kyōgen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} were performed in the castle kitchen{{sfn|館林市誌 歴史編|p=852}}. * The {{ill|Gohei|en|Gohei|qid=Q1011372|lt=gohei|1=Gohei}} bundles of the three shrines were entrusted to the {{ill|Yoriki|en|Yoriki|qid=Q3108649|lt=yōriki|1=Yoriki}} and presented to the town magistrate{{sfn|館林市誌 歴史編|p=852}}.
== Tennō Festival ==
=== The Tennō Festival of the Three Towns === The Tennō Festival, a festival of the Tennō shrines in three towns, has been held annually on {{ill|June 7 (lunar calendar)|ja|6月7日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/June 7 (lunar calendar)|1=June 7 (Lunisolar)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q839725}}{{sfn|館林市誌 歴史編|p=851}}.
==== Tennō Festival During the Rule of Tsunayoshi ==== According to the "Tatebayashi Machisaki no Shidai Memorandum" from {{ill|Hōei 5 (1708)|ja|宝永5年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōei 5 (1708)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135504462|1=1729}}, the Tennō Festival during the time when {{ill|Tokugawa Tsunayoshi|en|Tokugawa Tsunayoshi|qid=Q313745|lt=Tokugawa Tsunayoshi|1=Tokugawa Tsunayoshi}} was the lord of {{ill|Tatebayashi Domain|en|Tatebayashi Domain|qid=Q3034548|lt=Tatebayashi Domain|1=Tatebayashi Domain}} was as follows{{sfn|館林市誌 歴史編|pp=851-852}}:
* On {{ill|June 1 (Lunisolar)|ja|6月1日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/June 1 (Lunisolar)|qq=}}, seven days before the festival, materials were provided by the domain{{refnest|group="Note"|The materials from the domain consisted of six loads of green reed per shrine, 50 bamboo poles, four poles of five-sun circumference, and two {{ill|Gohei|en|Gohei|qid=Q1011372|lt=gohei|1=Gohei}} paper booklets per shrine{{sfn|館林市誌 歴史編|p=852}}. Since there were three shrines, three times this amount was actually supplied by the domain{{sfn|館林市誌 歴史編|p=852}}. The bamboo and green reed were used for making the roofs of temporary structures, and the pole bamboo was used for making the {{ill|Shishinhoko|ja|四神鉾|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shishinhoko|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a type of decorated spear with banners depicting the {{ill|Four Symbols|en|Four Symbols|qid=Q1073260|lt=Four Symbols|1=Four Symbols}} of Chinese mythology{{sfn|館林市誌 歴史編|p=852}}.}} and three {{ill|Otabisho|en|Otabisho|qid=Q11489117|lt=Otabisho|1=Otabisho}} were constructed in the town{{sfn|館林市誌 歴史編|pp=851-852}}. * On the festival day, the three shrines' {{ill|Mikoshi|en|Mikoshi|qid=Q979940|lt=mikoshi|1=Mikoshi}} were carried in a procession through the town, accompanied by {{ill|Yamabushi|en|Yamabushi|qid=Q2309838|lt=yamabushi|1=Yamabushi}}, {{ill|Miko|en|Miko|qid=Q1047139|lt=miko|1=Miko}}, {{ill|sacred horses|ja|神馬|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/sacred horses|1=Shinme|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11591047}}, {{ill|Cleyera japonica|en|Cleyera japonica|qid=Q775470|lt=sakaki|1=Cleyera japonica}}, {{ill|Lion dance|en|Lion dance|qid=Q713866|lt=shishi-mai|1=Lion dance}}, {{ill|Drum|en|Drum|qid=Q11404|lt=taiko|1=Drum}}, and {{ill|Matsuri float|en|Matsuri float|qid=Q11470974|lt=hoko|1=Matsuri float}}. The three mikoshi met at {{ill|Katamachi (Tatebayashi)|ja|片町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katamachi (Tatebayashi)|1=Katamachi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11569929}} {{sfn|館林市誌 歴史編|p=852}}. * The procession entered the {{ill|Ōtemon|ja|大手門|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtemon|1=ōte-mon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q5371967}}, crossed the {{ill|Sengan Bridge|ja|千貫橋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengan Bridge|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and proceeded into the castle, where the lord and castle administrator paid their respects, and {{ill|Kagura|en|Kagura|qid=Q1348260|lt=kagura|1=Kagura}} was offered{{sfn|館林市誌 歴史編|p=852}}. Offerings were given by the castle administrator{{refnest|group="Note"|The offerings included an initial harvest fee of one {{ill|kanmon|ja|貫文|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kanmon|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 貫|1=Japanese kan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q116911247}} per shrine, a sacred horse fee equivalent to two {{ill|hiki|ja|疋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hiki|1=hiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11578871}} per shrine, totaling three times that amount in reality{{sfn|館林市誌 歴史編|p=852}}. Additionally, the domain granted six {{ill|tawara|ja|俵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tawara|1=tawara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11386402}} of rice for offerings, two per shrine{{sfn|館林市誌 歴史編|p=852}}. Of these, three contained 3 {{ill|to|ja|斗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/to|1=To (unit)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1078168}} 7 {{ill|Sheng (volume)|en|Sheng (volume)|qid=Q995076|lt=shō|1=Sheng (volume)}} of glutinous rice{{sfn|館林市誌 歴史編|p=852}}. The glutinous rice was for offerings, while the regular rice was for food or making {{ill|Amazake|en|Amazake|qid=Q455990|lt=amazake|1=Amazake}} for the festival{{sfn|館林市誌 歴史編|p=852}}. The amount of these grants was highest during Tsunayoshi’s period and was significantly reduced under the {{ill|Ochi-Matsudaira clan|ja|越智松平家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ochi-Matsudaira clan|1=Ochi-Matsudaira clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11636553}}, although the festival continued in form until the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|en|Meiji Restoration|qid=Q8707|lt=Meiji Restoration|1=Meiji Restoration}}{{sfn|館林市誌 歴史編|p=852}}.}}{{sfn|館林市誌 歴史編|p=852}}. * After the lord's worship, the mikoshi left the castle and processed through the town{{sfn|館林市誌 歴史編|p=852}}.
=== Anecdotes ===
==== The Market Deity Taken as Collateral for Debt ==== An old document preserved in an old house in {{ill|Tatemachi|ja|竪町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tatemachi|1=Tatemachi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11598492}} records an interesting anecdote about the {{ill|Ichigami|ja|市神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichigami|qq=}} of {{ill|Renjaku Town|ja|連雀町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Renjaku Town|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 連雀|1=Renjaku Town|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11639950}}{{sfn|館林市誌 歴史編|p=848}}.
A man named Yogobei, who lived in Renjaku Town, had traditionally possessed the Ichigami. However, in {{ill|Genbun 2 (1737)|ja|元文2年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Genbun 2 (1737)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135504467|1=1737}}, he struggled to repay the temple offering fund to {{ill|Zendō-ji (Tatebayashi)|ja|善導寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zendō-ji (Tatebayashi)|1=Zendō-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q10923447}} and borrowed 12 ryō from 12 people within the same town, using the Ichigami as collateral{{sfn|館林市誌 歴史編|p=848}}. As he continued to struggle financially, he was forced to sell his house and consider moving to another town{{sfn|館林市誌 歴史編|p=848}}. To prevent the Ichigami from leaving Renjaku Town permanently, he negotiated with the 12 lenders, promising never to pledge the Ichigami again if they forgave his debt. The lenders agreed, and Yogobei successfully had his debt waived and regained the Ichigami{{sfn|館林市誌 歴史編|p=848}}.
==== The Bloodthirsty Lion Head Procession ====
The lion heads of the {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|en|Yasaka Shrine|qid=Q692714|lt=Yasaka Shrine (Tatebayashi)|1=Yasaka Shrine}} in {{ill|Sotokahōshi|ja|外加法師|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sotokahōshi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Sototomoki|ja|外伴木|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sototomoki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} had fierce expressions with glaring eyes and clenched teeth. The one in Sototomoki was particularly infamous, attributed to the spirit of {{ill|Hidari Jingorō|en|Hidari Jingorō|qid=Q2668220|lt=Hidari Jingorō|1=Hidari Jingorō}}, and was said to be so menacing that "it would not settle until it had seen human blood"{{sfn|尾曳之跡|p=36}}{{sfn|館林市誌 歴史編|p=869}}{{sfn|館林懐古|p=117}}. During the summer festival procession, the men carrying the lion head would charge through the town like a centipede, breaking into residences and causing havoc until blood was spilled, as the legend suggested{{sfn|尾曳之跡|p=36}}{{sfn|館林懐古|p=117}}. Despite the chaos, the domain authorities regarded these acts as "divine will" and did not intervene{{sfn|尾曳之跡|p=36}}. == Abolished Shrines and Their Aftermath == This section describes the current status of the Tennō shrines after their abolition.
=== Renjaku Kosodate Kannon === {{infobox shrine | name = Renjaku Kosodate Kannon | native_name = 連雀子育観音 | religious_affiliation = {{ill|Shinto|en|Shinto|qid=Q812767|lt=Shinto|1=Shinto}} | image = [[File:Renjaku Kosodate Kannon at Renjaku-cho, Tatebayashi.jpg|240px]]<br/>Renjaku Kosodate Kannon, believed to be at the former site of the Ichigami in Renjaku Town | location = {{ill|Tatebayashi, Gunma|en|Tatebayashi, Gunma|qid=Q858016|lt=Tatebayashi, Gunma|1=Tatebayashi, Gunma}}, Honmachi 1-3 | coordinates = {{coord|36|14|52.77|N|139|32|9.04|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline}} }} {{commonscat|Renjaku Kosodate Kannon|連雀子育観音}}
{{ill|Ichigami|ja|市神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichigami|qq=}} in {{ill|Renjaku Town|ja|連雀町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Renjaku Town|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 連雀|1=Renjaku Town|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11639950}} was transferred early to the three towns, but at its former site remains a small hall called "Renjaku Kosodate Kannon" to this day{{sfn|神社ぐだぐだ参拝録}}. According to tradition, this Kannon Hall, originally within the grounds of the Ichigami, was widely revered by locals for its blessings in raising healthy and intelligent children. Due to popular demand, it remained at its location even after the Ichigami was relocated{{sfn|神社ぐだぐだ参拝録}}. The hall was well known, as evidenced by references to it in the transition documents from {{ill|Inoue Masaharu|en|Inoue Masaharu|qid=Q8013036|lt=Inoue Masaharu|1=Inoue Masaharu}} to {{ill|Akimoto Tomotomo|ja|秋元志朝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akimoto Tomotomo|1=Akimoto Yukitomo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q8515291}} in {{ill|Kōka 3 (1846)|ja|弘化3年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōka 3 (1846)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135504473|1=1846}}{{sfn|神社ぐだぐだ参拝録}}.
=== Impact of Shrine Consolidation === The three-town {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|en|Yasaka Shrine|qid=Q692714|lt=Yasaka Shrine|1=Yasaka Shrine}}, which was widely worshipped throughout the Edo period, was abolished during the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|qid=Q3125096|lt=Meiji period|1=Meiji era}} due to the {{ill|Shrine Consolidation Policy|en|Shrine Consolidation Policy|qid=Q11590700|lt=Shrine Consolidation Policy|1=Shrine Consolidation Policy}}, which saw it absorbed into surrounding shrines. Similarly, the two Yasaka Shrines transferred from Yamagata were also abolished through merger.
==== Merger with Ōme Shrine in Tanigoshimachi ==== The Yasaka Shrine in {{ill|Tanigoshimachi|ja|谷越町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanigoshimachi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} was officially merged into the neighboring {{ill|Ōme Shrine|ja|青梅神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōme Shrine|1=Aoume Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q80835943}} on {{ill|October 25, 1910|ja|1910th year 10月25日|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/October 25, 1910|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}{{sfn|館林の社寺|p=35}}.
==== Merger with Nagara Shrine in Daikanmachi ==== {{infobox shrine | name = Kotohira Shrine | native_name = 琴平神社 | religious_affiliation = {{ill|Shinto|en|Shinto|qid=Q812767|lt=Shinto|1=Shinto}} | image = [[File:Hondo at Kotohira Shirine, Tatebayashi.jpg|270px]]<br/>Kotohira Shrine, a former auxiliary shrine of Ashikagacho's Tennō Shrine, now restored to its original location | location = {{ill|Tatebayashi, Gunma|en|Tatebayashi, Gunma|qid=Q858016|lt=Tatebayashi, Gunma|1=Tatebayashi, Gunma}}, Honmachi 1-8-47 | deity = {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞鳴命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スサノオ}} | coordinates = {{coord|36|15|1.19|N|139|32|7.08|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline}} }} {{commonscat|Kotohira-jinja (Tatebayashi)|琴平神社}}
The Yasaka Shrines of {{ill|Ashikagacho|ja|足利町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashikagacho|1=Ashikagacho|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q97378068}} and {{ill|Tatemachi|ja|竪町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tatemachi|1=Tatemachi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11598492}} were merged, along with their auxiliary shrines, into {{ill|Nagara Shrine (Tatebayashi Daikanmachi)|ja|長良神社 (館林市代官町)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagara Shrine (Tatebayashi Daikanmachi)|1=Nagara Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11653646}} in {{ill|Daikanmachi (Tatebayashi)|ja|代官町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daikanmachi (Tatebayashi)|1=Daikanmachi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q17503091}} on {{ill|October 21, 1910|ja|1910th year 10月21日|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/October 21, 1910|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}{{sfn|館林市誌 歴史編|p=864}}. One of the auxiliary shrines, {{ill|Kotohira shrines|lt=Kotohira shrines|lt=Kotohira Shrine|ja|琴平神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kotohira Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 金刀比羅神社}}, was later restored to its original location due to the devotion of its worshippers{{sfn|館林の社寺|p=13}}.
==== Merger with Obiki Inari Shrine ==== The Yasaka Shrines of {{ill|Sotokahōshi|ja|外加法師|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sotokahōshi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Sototomoki|ja|外伴木|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sototomoki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, transferred from Yamagata, became difficult to maintain after the {{ill|Abolition of the han system|en|Abolition of the han system|qid=Q331905|lt=Abolition of Domains and Establishment of Prefectures|1=Abolition of the han system}}. They were officially merged into {{ill|Obiki Inari Shrine|ja|尾曳稲荷神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Obiki Inari Shrine|1=Obiki Inari Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q80428331}} on {{ill|November 14, 1910|ja|1910th year 11月14日|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/November 14, 1910|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}{{sfn|尾曳之跡|p=35}}{{sfn|館林の社寺|p=26}}.
=== The Fate of the Festival ===
==== The Tennō Festival of the Three Towns ==== Even after the three-town Yasaka Shrines were merged, the summer festival originating from the Tennō Festival continued. The procession of festival floats was observed until the early {{ill|Shōwa period|ja|昭和時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōwa period|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 昭和|1=Shōwa era|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q327567}}{{sfn|館林市歴史文化基本構想|p=14}}. In {{ill|1975|en|1975|qid=Q2479|lt=1975 (Shōwa 50)|1=1975}}, the city's summer festivals were unified into the "Tatebayashi Festival" ({{ill|Tatebayashi Matsuri|ja|館林まつり|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tatebayashi Matsuri|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), held annually on the third Sunday of July and the preceding Saturday. The festival now features a folk dance parade and an adult mikoshi procession along Honmachi Street{{sfn|館林市歴史文化基本構想|p=14}}.
==== The Procession of the Lion Heads ==== Due to years of use, the lion heads of the Yasaka Shrines in Sotokahōshi and Sototomoki had deteriorated significantly. Around {{ill|1887|en|1887|qid=Q7826|lt=1887 (Meiji 20)|1=1887}}, new replicas were commissioned from {{ill|Sōmei Harumitsu|ja|五月女春光|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōmei Harumitsu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a sculptor from Renjaku Town{{sfn|館林市誌 歴史編|p=869}}{{sfn|館林懐古|p=117}}. This suggests that the festival continued at least until that period. It is uncertain whether the lion head from Sotokahōshi or Sototomoki was preserved, but one of them was transferred to Obiki Inari Shrine along with the shrine's merger and remains a cultural artifact to this day{{sfn|館林市誌 歴史編|p=869}}.
===
== 所在location map ==
=== 秋元時代の古location map === [[File:Tenno Shrines in Tatebayashi at old map.png|800px|none|館林の天王社所在location map、秋元家の藩主時代]]
==== 部分拡大 ==== <gallery> File:Yasaka Shrine on old map at Zaimoku-cho, Tatebayashi.png|[[=竪町の天王|竪町の天王]]。別当である法性院(location map中では宝性イン)も見える。 File:Yasaka Shrine on old map at Yagoe-cho, Tatebayashi.png|[[=谷越町の天王|谷越町の天王]]。別当である円蔵院の他、南に接する宝幢寺内には[[青梅神社]]も見える。 File:Yasaka Shrine on old map at Ashikaga-cho, Tatebayashi.png|[[=足利町の天王|足利町の天王]]。別当である密蔵院も見える。 File:Yasaka Shrine on old map at SotoKahoshi, Tatebayashi.png|[[=外加法師の八坂神社|外加法師の八坂神社]]。 File:Yasaka Shrine on old map at SotoBangi, Tatebayashi.png|[[=外伴木の八坂神社|外伴木の八坂神社]]。 </gallery>
=== 現代location map === [[File:Tenno Shrines in Tatebayashi at OSM.png|800px|none|館林の天王社所在location map、現代]]

== Bibliography == * {{Cite book|和書|title=館林市誌 歴史編|editor=館林市誌編集委員会|publisher=[[館林市役所]]|year=1969|ref={{sfnref|館林市誌 歴史編}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|title=館林市史 普及版 館林の歴史|editor=館林市史編さん委員会|publisher=[[館林市]]|year=2019|ref={{sfnref|館林市史 普及版}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|title=館林懐古|author=川島維知|publisher=館林地方史研究会|year=1976|ref={{sfnref|館林懐古}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|title=尾曳之跡 館林史蹟総覧|author=高橋坤二|publisher=歴史図書社|year=1979|ref={{sfnref|尾曳之跡}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|title=館林文庫 館林の話|author=福田啓作|publisher=国書刊行会|year=1980|ref={{sfnref|館林の話}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|title=館林双書15 館林の社寺|author=川島維知|publisher=館林市立図書館|year=1986|ref={{sfnref|館林の社寺}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|title=館林双書21 福田啓作郷土資料|author=福田啓作|publisher=館林市立図書館|year=1993|ref={{sfnref|福田啓作郷土資料}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|title=邑楽館林史帖|author=青木源作|publisher=みやま文庫|year=1994|ref={{sfnref|邑楽館林史帖}}}} * {{Cite journal|和書|author=関戸明子|coauthors=木部一幸|title=館林城下の Municipal History的変遷と地域構成|journal=歴史地理学|volume=40|issue=4|pages=19-37|date=1998-09|naid=40004341522|ref={{sfnref|館林城下の Municipal History的変遷と地域構成}}}} * {{Cite web|和書|url=http://glassesmaiden.blog81.fc2.com/blog-date-20170918.html|title=連雀子育観音（本町一丁目）|publisher=神社ぐだぐだ参拝録|accessdate=2020-05-29|date=2017-09-18|ref={{sfnref|神社ぐだぐだ参拝録}}}} * {{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.city.tatebayashi.gunma.jp/docs/2019032800043/files/all.pdf|title=館林市歴史文化基本構想|publisher=館林市文化振興課|accessdate=2020-05-29|date=2019-03-28|ref={{sfnref|館林市歴史文化基本構想}}}}
